Human Use.short_desc: Research methodology involving human participants as the primary subjects of inquiry, employing qualitative, quantitative, or mixed-methods approaches to generate knowledge about human behavior, experience, perception, cognition, attitudes, identity, social dynamics, or cultural practices. Data collection is conducted through a wide range of instruments and techniques, including in-depth individual interviews, semi-structured or unstructured interviews, structured surveys, self-administered paper-based or online questionnaires, focus group discussions, dyadic or group interviews, direct non-participant observation, participant observation, ethnographic fieldwork, diary or journal methods, vignette studies, document analysis, and secondary analysis of existing human-generated data. Research may be exploratory, descriptive, explanatory, or evaluative in nature, and may adopt inductive, deductive, or abductive reasoning frameworks depending on the epistemological stance of the researcher. Studies are typically situated within disciplines such as sociology, psychology, education, public health, communication, political science, anthropology, social work, gender studies, cultural studies, or interdisciplinary fields. Participant populations are diverse and may include students at primary, secondary, or tertiary levels; academic faculty and university staff; employees and managers in professional or organizational settings; healthcare workers or patients; community members from specific geographic or demographic groups; marginalized or underrepresented populations; online community members; and individuals identified by shared experiences, occupational roles, gender identities, sexual orientations, racial or ethnic backgrounds, religious affiliations, or disability status. Research settings include university campuses, schools, hospitals, workplaces, community centers, public spaces, and digital or virtual environments such as social media platforms, online forums, and video conferencing tools. All research involving human subjects is governed by robust ethical frameworks and institutional protocols, including the submission and approval of study protocols by an Institutional Review Board (IRB), Research Ethics Committee (REC), or equivalent ethics oversight body. Researchers must obtain voluntary, informed, written or verbal consent from all participants or their legal guardians prior to data collection, ensure full anonymity or confidentiality of participant data, provide the right to withdraw without penalty at any stage, minimize risks of psychological or social harm, and maintain data security in compliance with applicable privacy regulations such as GDPR or national data protection laws. Data analysis approaches may include thematic analysis, content analysis, grounded theory coding, narrative analysis, discourse analysis, interpretive phenomenological analysis (IPA), framework analysis, statistical analysis using descriptive or inferential methods, regression modeling, structural equation modeling (SEM), factor analysis, or network analysis, depending on the research design and data type. Findings are reported in ways that protect participant anonymity, contextualize lived experiences, and contribute meaningfully to theory, policy, or practice in the relevant field.

Animal Welfare.short_desc: Research methodology involving live animals as experimental subjects in controlled laboratory, vivarium, animal facility, farm, or field settings, with explicit attention to the ethical treatment, housing conditions, physiological welfare, and humane care of all animals used throughout the study. Research encompasses a broad range of experimental designs including nutritional feeding trials, dietary supplementation studies, growth performance assessments, reproductive physiology studies, behavioral and ethological observations, pharmacological and toxicological evaluations, surgical interventions, immunological assays, pathological examinations, and biomedical or veterinary research. Animal subjects may include rodents such as rats, mice, guinea pigs, and hamsters; rabbits; avian species such as broiler and layer chickens, ducks, quail, and turkeys; aquatic animals such as tilapia, catfish, salmon, shrimp, and zebrafish; large agricultural animals such as pigs, goats, sheep, and cattle; companion animals; and non-human primates used in biomedical research. All research protocols are developed and implemented in strict accordance with the 3Rs framework — Replacement, which requires justification for the use of animals over non-animal alternatives; Reduction, which minimizes the number of animals used while maintaining statistical power; and Refinement, which ensures procedures are modified to minimize pain, suffering, and distress while improving animal welfare. Prior to commencement of any experimental work, formal approval must be obtained from the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC), Animal Ethics Committee (AEC), or equivalent national regulatory body. All documentation must include complete descriptions of species, strain, age, sex, and number of animals; housing and environmental enrichment conditions; dietary regimens; experimental procedures and their justification; anesthesia and analgesia protocols; pain and distress monitoring criteria; and defined humane endpoints at which animals will be removed from experiments or euthanized using approved, painless methods. Throughout the study, trained personnel conduct regular health monitoring, veterinary checks, and welfare assessments to detect and respond promptly to any signs of abnormal behavior, pain, illness, injury, or deterioration in body condition. Data collected from animal studies may include body weight gain, feed intake and feed conversion ratio, organ weights and organ-to-body-weight ratios, hematological and biochemical blood parameters, serum or plasma hormone and metabolite levels, histopathological examination of tissues, microbial analyses of gut contents, behavioral metrics from standardized tests, and mortality or survival rates. Statistical analyses compare treatment groups using appropriate parametric or non-parametric methods, accounting for animal-level and cage-level variation. All results are interpreted in the context of animal welfare implications and the generalizability of findings to target species or biomedical applications.
must_keywords: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee

Plant Use.short_desc: Research methodology involving plants, crops, botanical specimens, or agricultural systems as the primary subjects of study, conducted in field, greenhouse, screenhouse, growth chamber, nursery, or controlled laboratory environments. Research covers the full spectrum of plant science and agricultural inquiry, including agronomy, horticulture, plant physiology, plant pathology, plant breeding and genetics, soil science, agroecology, post-harvest technology, and ethnobotany. Experimental procedures encompass the complete agricultural or botanical research cycle: seed sourcing, selection, and pre-treatment such as scarification or priming; germination testing; seedling production and nursery management; land preparation and plot establishment; transplanting or direct sowing; systematic application of experimental treatments including varying rates of mineral fertilizers, organic amendments, biostimulants, plant growth regulators, irrigation regimes, mulching materials, pesticides, herbicides, fungicides, and biocontrol agents; phenological recording and growth monitoring at defined vegetative and reproductive stages; measurement of morphological, physiological, and biochemical plant parameters; systematic scouting and recording of pest, disease, and weed incidence and severity; harvesting procedures; and quantitative yield, biomass, and quality assessments. Studies may also investigate plant responses to abiotic stress factors including drought, salinity, extreme temperatures, waterlogging, heavy metal toxicity, and elevated CO2 levels, or biotic stresses caused by fungal, bacterial, viral, nematode, insect, or weed pressures. Experimental designs follow recognized statistical frameworks such as the Completely Randomized Design (CRD), Randomized Complete Block Design (RCBD), Latin Square Design, split-plot design, strip-plot design, or factorial arrangements, with clearly defined treatment structures, replication levels, and randomization procedures. Plot layout, row spacing, plant density, and buffer zones are explicitly described. Agronomic management practices including irrigation scheduling, fertilization timing and rates, integrated pest management protocols, mechanical or chemical weed control, and canopy management are standardized across all experimental units. Data collection covers quantitative agronomic parameters such as germination rate and percentage, seedling vigor, plant height, tiller number, leaf area index, canopy cover, days to flowering and maturity, grain or fruit number per plant, grain weight, harvest index, total biological yield, and economic yield. Physiological and biochemical measurements may include photosynthesis rate, stomatal conductance, transpiration rate, chlorophyll content, chlorophyll fluorescence, water use efficiency, relative water content, proline accumulation, antioxidant enzyme activity, and nutrient concentration in plant tissues. Soil parameters relevant to plant growth such as pH, organic matter, available nutrient levels, microbial biomass, and bulk density may also be measured. Statistical analysis employs analysis of variance (ANOVA), means separation using Tukey's HSD, Duncan's Multiple Range Test (DMRT), or LSD at defined significance levels, along with regression, correlation, and multivariate analyses where appropriate.

Microbiological/Biotechnological Use.short_desc: Research methodology involving the use, manipulation, characterization, or application of microorganisms, cell cultures, biological molecules, viral agents, or genetically modified organisms in laboratory or controlled biocontainment settings. Research spans the full breadth of microbiology and biotechnology, including bacteriology, virology, mycology, parasitology, phycology, environmental microbiology, industrial microbiology, medical microbiology, food microbiology, agricultural microbiology, molecular biology, cell biology, genomics, proteomics, metabolomics, and synthetic biology. Experimental procedures encompass sample collection and preparation; isolation and purification of target microorganisms; culture media formulation including preparation of selective, differential, enrichment, and minimal media in agar or broth formats; aseptic inoculation; incubation under defined conditions of temperature, humidity, CO2 level, aeration, and light; subculturing and maintenance of stock cultures; colony counting and enumeration; identification and characterization using morphological, biochemical, physiological, and molecular techniques; susceptibility testing; fermentation in batch, fed-batch, or continuous mode; upstream and downstream bioprocessing; genetic manipulation including transformation, conjugation, transduction, and gene editing; protein expression, purification, and characterization; and development of bioassays, biosensors, or diagnostic tools. Studies investigating antimicrobial agents must describe disk diffusion, broth dilution, or agar dilution assays and report Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) and Minimum Bactericidal or Fungicidal Concentration (MBC/MFC) values. Research involving recombinant DNA technology, genetically modified organisms, viral vectors, select agents, or potentially pathogenic microorganisms requires formal documentation of biosafety committee approval, institutional biosafety committee (IBC) clearance, applicable biosafety level classification (BSL-1 through BSL-4), risk group assignment, containment procedures, personal protective equipment requirements, decontamination and waste disposal protocols, emergency response procedures, and compliance with national and international biosafety regulations such as the Cartagena Protocol, NIH guidelines, or WHO laboratory biosafety manual. Gene editing approaches using CRISPR-Cas9, TALEN, zinc finger nucleases, or other platforms must describe target gene selection, guide RNA design, delivery mechanism, off-target assessment, and validation of editing outcomes. Genomic and metagenomic studies describe DNA or RNA extraction protocols, library preparation, sequencing platform, bioinformatic pipeline, and databases used for sequence annotation and comparative analysis. Proteomics or metabolomics studies describe sample preparation, chromatographic separation, mass spectrometry platform, data acquisition parameters, and statistical approaches for differential expression analysis. All culture collections, biological materials, and data generated are managed in accordance with institutional policies on biological material transfer, intellectual property, and research data management.

Engineering.short_desc: Research methodology involving the systematic design, development, simulation, fabrication, assembly, testing, optimization, and evaluation of mechanical, electrical, electronic, civil, structural, chemical, environmental, industrial, or interdisciplinary engineering systems, devices, components, or processes. Research may span fundamental engineering science investigations, applied engineering development, technology proof-of-concept demonstrations, or comparative performance evaluations of competing engineering solutions. Study areas include but are not limited to structural and geotechnical engineering, fluid mechanics and hydraulics, thermodynamics and heat transfer, machine design and mechanisms, manufacturing and production engineering, materials science and metallurgy, control systems and automation, power systems and electrical machines, electronics and embedded systems, mechatronics and robotics, renewable energy systems, environmental and water resources engineering, transportation and infrastructure engineering, and biomedical engineering. The research process typically follows iterative design cycles beginning with problem identification and requirements analysis; conceptual design and feasibility assessment; detailed engineering design using computer-aided design tools such as AutoCAD, SolidWorks, CATIA, or ANSYS; mathematical modeling and simulation using finite element analysis (FEA), computational fluid dynamics (CFD), multibody dynamics, or circuit simulation; physical prototyping using machining, casting, forging, sheet metal forming, additive manufacturing or 3D printing, welding, or composite fabrication; system integration and assembly; sensor installation and instrumentation; calibration of measurement equipment; controlled experimental testing under defined loading, thermal, environmental, or operational conditions; systematic data acquisition using data loggers, oscilloscopes, spectrum analyzers, or custom data acquisition systems; statistical analysis of performance data; and iterative design refinement based on experimental outcomes. Testing protocols explicitly define test configurations, boundary conditions, applied loads or inputs, measurement points, sampling rates, and acceptance criteria. Materials characterization may involve tensile testing, hardness testing, impact testing, fatigue testing, corrosion assessment, microstructural analysis using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) or optical microscopy, X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), or differential scanning calorimetry (DSC). Electrical and electronic systems are evaluated for voltage, current, power, efficiency, frequency response, signal-to-noise ratio, impedance, and electromagnetic compatibility. Fluid systems are assessed for flow rate, pressure drop, velocity profiles, turbulence characteristics, heat transfer coefficients, and pump or turbine performance. Structural systems are evaluated for static and dynamic load-carrying capacity, deformation, natural frequency, damping ratio, and failure modes. All engineering research is benchmarked against applicable international standards such as ASTM, ISO, IEEE, ASME, or IEC standards, and findings are interpreted in terms of engineering design implications, safety factors, and potential for practical deployment or technology transfer.

Information Technology Use.short_desc: Research methodology involving the design, development, implementation, analysis, testing, evaluation, or optimization of software systems, digital applications, computational algorithms, data architectures, information systems, communication networks, cybersecurity frameworks, or emerging digital technologies. Research spans multiple sub-disciplines including software engineering, computer science, data science and analytics, artificial intelligence and machine learning, human-computer interaction, information systems, network engineering, database management, cybersecurity and information assurance, cloud and distributed computing, the Internet of Things, digital signal processing, and emerging areas such as blockchain, quantum computing, and extended reality. The research process may follow established software development methodologies such as Agile, Scrum, Kanban, Waterfall, DevOps, Rapid Application Development, or the Design Science Research Methodology (DSRM), and involves distinct phases including requirements elicitation and specification, system architecture and design, implementation and coding, integration and deployment, testing and quality assurance, and performance evaluation. Artificial intelligence and machine learning studies describe the problem formulation, dataset sourcing and characteristics, preprocessing pipeline including normalization, augmentation, imputation, and feature engineering; model architecture selection and rationale; training procedure including optimizer, learning rate, batch size, number of epochs, and hardware specifications; regularization and hyperparameter tuning strategy; validation approach such as k-fold cross-validation, holdout, or leave-one-out; and performance evaluation using domain-appropriate metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1 score, AUC-ROC, mean absolute error, root mean squared error, BLEU score, or ROUGE score. Cybersecurity research describes the threat model, attack surface, vulnerability assessment methodology, penetration testing approach, tools and frameworks used, and evaluation of countermeasures or detection systems. Network research describes topology, protocols, traffic generation, simulation tools, and performance metrics. Database research describes schema design, query optimization, indexing strategies, and scalability evaluation. Human-computer interaction research describes user study protocols, interface design rationale, usability evaluation methods, accessibility compliance, and user experience metrics. System evaluation employs both objective quantitative metrics and subjective quality assessments, and results are benchmarked against existing state-of-the-art systems or baseline approaches. Research outputs may include functional software prototypes, trained and validated models, curated datasets, APIs, technical frameworks, design patterns, or evidence-based guidelines for system development and deployment. All research involving human users in usability or user experience studies follows ethical protocols for human participant research, and data privacy considerations comply with applicable regulations such as GDPR, CCPA, or national data privacy laws.

Food Technology Use.short_desc: Research methodology involving the scientific study of food raw materials, ingredients, processing technologies, preservation methods, product formulation, quality systems, safety assessment, packaging, and nutritional or functional properties of food products. Research encompasses the full continuum from raw material characterization and ingredient sourcing through processing and manufacturing to final product evaluation and shelf-life determination. Disciplinary areas include food chemistry, food microbiology, food processing technology, food engineering, sensory science, nutritional biochemistry, food packaging science, post-harvest technology, food safety and hygiene, product development, and nutraceutical or functional food research. Experimental procedures begin with raw material procurement, characterization, and pre-treatment, followed by systematic formulation of food products varying in ingredient composition, processing parameters, or packaging conditions. Processing techniques applied in research may include thermal treatments such as pasteurization, sterilization, blanching, and ultra-high temperature (UHT) processing; non-thermal treatments such as high-pressure processing (HPP), pulsed electric field (PEF), ultraviolet irradiation, cold plasma, and ozone treatment; drying and dehydration methods such as hot-air drying, spray drying, freeze drying, vacuum drying, and osmotic dehydration; fermentation using selected or wild microbial cultures; extrusion; emulsification and homogenization; encapsulation techniques; and smoking or curing. Product development studies describe the formulation rationale, ingredient interactions, process optimization approach, scale-up considerations, and sensory acceptability benchmarks. Sensory evaluation is conducted using trained panels, semi-trained panels, or consumer panels employing established methods such as descriptive analysis, difference testing (triangle test, duo-trio test), preference ranking, paired comparison, or hedonic scaling using structured 9-point or visual analog scales. Physicochemical analyses describe measurement of moisture content, water activity (aw), pH, titratable acidity, color using colorimetric instruments (CIE L*a*b*), texture profile analysis (TPA), viscosity, emulsion stability, gelation properties, particle size distribution, and rheological behavior. Proximate composition analysis follows standardized methods such as AOAC, AOAC International, or ISO methods to quantify moisture, crude protein, crude fat, crude fiber, ash, and carbohydrate content. Nutritional and bioactive compound analysis employs chromatographic techniques including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), gas chromatography (GC), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), or gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) to quantify vitamins, minerals, phenolic compounds, flavonoids, carotenoids, fatty acid profiles, amino acid composition, and other bioactive molecules. Antioxidant capacity is assessed using assays such as DPPH, ABTS, FRAP, or ORAC. Microbiological safety and stability assessments involve total plate count, coliform enumeration, yeast and mold count, and specific pathogen testing for organisms such as Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, E. coli O157:H7, Staphylococcus aureus, and Campylobacter, following international standards such as ISO, FDA BAM, or USDA FSIS methods. Shelf-life studies are conducted under defined storage conditions of temperature, relative humidity, and light exposure, with periodic sampling to monitor changes in physicochemical, sensory, microbiological, and nutritional parameters over time. Packaging research evaluates mechanical properties, barrier properties against oxygen, moisture vapor, and light, migration testing, and sustainability characteristics of packaging materials. Research is conducted in compliance with food safety management systems including Hazard Analysis and Critical Control Points (HACCP), Good Manufacturing Practice (GMP), Good Hygiene Practice (GHP), ISO 22000, and applicable national food safety regulations. All sensory studies involving human panelists obtain informed consent and comply with institutional ethics requirements.
